flashcards.back: The systematic process of identifying, documenting, validating, and managing the requirements of a software system. It forms the foundation of successful software development.
Specific functions/features the system must perform, such as user interactions, data processing, and system operations. They describe what the system should do.
Quality attributes that specify how the system performs certain functions, including performance, security, reliability, usability, and scalability.
The process of gathering requirements from stakeholders through techniques like interviews, surveys, observations, prototyping, and workshops.
The process of studying stakeholder needs to define system requirements, identifying conflicts, feasibility issues, and creating models to understand requirements better.
A description of system behavior from a user's perspective that shows interactions between actors and the system to achieve specific goals.
A comprehensive document that describes the system's functionality, performance, and constraints. It serves as a contract between developers and stakeholders.
The process of ensuring that the documented requirements accurately represent stakeholder needs and are complete, consistent, and testable.
The process of managing changes to requirements throughout the project lifecycle, maintaining traceability, and ensuring requirements remain aligned with business goals.
An initial assessment to determine whether the proposed project is technically, economically, and operationally feasible before proceeding with full requirements gathering.
The ability to trace requirements throughout the development lifecycle, linking requirements to design, code, and test cases to ensure completeness.
A short, simple description of a feature from the end-user's perspective, typically following the format: As a [user], I want [goal] so that [benefit].

flashcards.front: What are the 5 levels of the Requirements Engineering process?
flashcards.back: 1) Feasibility Study, 2) Requirements Elicitation and Analysis, 3) Requirements Specification, 4) Requirements Validation, 5) Requirements Management.
Any individual, group, or organization that has an interest in the software system, including users, customers, developers, managers, and external parties.

flashcards.front: What is Prototyping in requirements engineering?
flashcards.back: Creating an early, simplified version of the system to help stakeholders visualize requirements, validate assumptions, and discover additional requirements through feedback.
